sales.title:
Ancients
Augustus (27 BC-AD 14). AR denarius (19mm, 3.84 gm, 7h). NGC XF S 5/5 - 4/5. Spain (Colonia Patricia?), ca. 18-16 BC. Bare head of Augustus right, linear border / AVGVSTVS, Capricorn right, holding globe attached to rudder, bearing cornucopia on back; linear border. RIC I 126. Onyx toning with mirror-like surfaces. After visiting the astrologer Theogenes at Apollonia around 45/44 BC with his friend Agrippa, Augustus (then Octavian) became more assured of his destined greatness. Suetonius, the ancient Roman historian, mentions that Augustus, inspired by the astrological significance of his birth under the sign of Capricorn, issued a silver coin bearing this sign. This coinage symbolically represented not only Augustus's belief in the protective and auspicious power of Capricorn but also served as a potent emblem of his destined leadership and the commencement of a new era of Roman rule under his auspices. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

Augustus (27 BC-AD 14). AR denarius (21mm, 3.49 gm, 7h). NGC XF 5/5 - 4/5. Uncertain Spanish mint (Colonia Caesaraugusta?), ca. 19-18 BC. Laureate head of Augustus left / CAESAR / AVGVSTVS, two laurel trees/branches. RIC I 33b. Splashes of aqua, peach, and violet toning. Ex Robert W. Hubel Collection (acquired in the 1930s). The trees featured on this reverse type are a representation of the laurel trees gifted to Octavian by the Senate when they bestowed the title of Augustus upon him. Ancients
Octavian, as Imperator (ca. 33-30 BC). AR denarius (20mm, 3.79 gm, 12h). NGC VF 5/5 - 5/5. Uncertain Italian mint (Rome or Brundisium?), ca. 31-30 BC. Diademed half-length bust of Victory right, wearing pendant earring / CAESAR-DIVI F, Neptune, nude save for cloak over shoulders, standing left, right foot on globe, grounded scepter in left hand, aplustre in outstretched right hand. RIC I 256. RSC 60. BMC 615. CBN 12. A swirl of navy and sunflower toning. Ex Andrew McCabe Collection (Paul-Francis Jacquier, Auction 51-52, 15 September 2023), lot 499. Born on 23 September 63 BC into a wealthy plebeian family, Augustus, originally Gaius Octavius, was the grandnephew and posthumously adopted son of Julius Caesar. This inheritance set him on a collision course with Rome's political elite, leading to the formation of the Second Triumvirate with Marc Antony and Marcus Aemilius Lepidus. Their initial unity against Caesar's assassins eventually gave way to conflict, from which Augustus emerged as Rome's first true emperor after victories in key battles like Philippi and Actium. In 27 BC, he was honored with the title "Augustus" by the Senate, signaling the start of his reign and the Pax Romana, a golden era of peace, prosperity, and cultural flourishing that lasted over two centuries. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

sales.title:
Augustus (27 BC-AD 14), with L. Mescinius Rufus, as moneyer. AR denarius (18mm, 3.91 gm, 10h). NGC Choice XF S 5/5 - 4/5. Rome, ca. 16 BC. Laureate head of Augustus right / L MESCINI-VS RVFVS, Mars standing facing, helmeted head left, nude save for cloak, spear in lowered right hand, parazonium cradled in left arm, standing on pedestal inscribed S P Q R / V PR RE / CAES in three lines. RIC I 351. Subtle pearl-like toning. Ex Heritage Auctions, Auction 271993 (18 August 2019), lot 35105. Born on 23 September 63 BC into a wealthy plebeian family, Augustus, originally Gaius Octavius, was the grandnephew and posthumously adopted son of Julius Caesar. This inheritance set him on a collision course with Rome's political elite, leading to the formation of the Second Triumvirate with Marc Antony and Marcus Aemilius Lepidus. Their initial unity against Caesar's assassins eventually gave way to conflict, from which Augustus emerged as Rome's first true emperor after victories in key battles like Philippi and Actium. In 27 BC, he was honored with the title "Augustus" by the Senate, signaling the start of his reign and the Pax Romana, a golden era of peace, prosperity, and cultural flourishing that lasted over two centuries. Augustus' rule saw significant military, administrative, and cultural reforms that stabilized and enriched the empire. He established a standing army, including the Praetorian Guard, reorganized Rome's provinces, and became a patron of the arts, fostering the careers of poets like Virgil and Horace and commissioning monumental buildings that reshaped Rome. His savvy use of coinage to communicate his achievements and vision helped solidify his power and legacy. Augustus died in AD 14, he left behind a transformed Rome. Through Suetonius's "The Twelve Caesars" and his own "Res Gestae Divi Augusti," we glimpse the life and achievements of a ruler who balanced autocracy with republican ideals, setting a precedent for future emperors and leaving an indelible mark on Roman history. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

sales.title: Augustus (27 BC-AD 14). AR denarius (18mm, 3.80 gm, 11h). NGC Choice VF 4/5 - 3/5, brushed. Lugdunum, 8 BC. AVGVSTVS-DIVI•F, laureate head of Augustus right / C•CAES / AVGVS•F, Gaius Caesar on horse leaping right, reins in right hand, sword and shield in left; aquila between two standards beyond. RIC I 199.  In 8 BC, Augustus introduced his twelve-year-old grandson and adopted son Gaius Caesar to the Roman army in Gaul. The bulla around Gaius' neck signifies that he was still a child and had not yet assumed the toga of manhood (J. Pollini, Gaius Caesar on horseback, Museum Notes 30, 1985). sales.title: Octavian, as Imperator and Triumvir (43-33 BC). AR denarius (20mm, 3.51 gm, 4h). NGC Choice VF 5/5 - 1/5, scratches, brushed, smoothing. Commemorating the capture of Egypt, uncertain Eastern mint, ca. 28 BC. CAESAR•DIVI•F-COS•VI, bare head of Augustus right; capricorn right below truncation / AEGVPTO / CAPTA, crocodile advancing right. RIC I 545.   This coin is of the utmost historical importance, because it refers to the consolidation of the Roman power and reach, under the control of Augustus. Augustus accomplished this with his victory over Marc Antony and Cleopatra at Actium and their subsequent suicides. The crocodile, a recognizable symbol of Egypt, combined with this simple two-word legend, AEGYPTO CAPTA, refers to the subjugation of Ptolemaic Egypt and its incorporation as an Imperial province under Rome, and thus the Empire was born.   HID05401242017  © 2026 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
